Route F examines sections. Columbo traces scenarios. OOB challenges assumptions. None of these naturally read the full document linearly. The coherence check must be a distinct step that reads top-to-bottom, not a byproduct of other analysis phases. This is why it was missed in Pass 1 and caught in Pass 2.
entries.reuse_signal: when adversarial analysis passes but the document still has contradictions
entries.intent: Understand why per-section analysis misses cross-section contradictions

entries.content: Deep work pipeline on a single-script feature felt like overkill but caught real issues: long response handling would have been wrong (claimed LLM summarization that wasn't feasible), ANSI stripping had a fragile filter, multi-turn limitation needed explicit framing. The quality loop's value is proportional to the number of assumptions in the design, not the size of the output.
entries.intent: Calibrate when deep work is worth the overhead
entries.reuse_signal: questioning whether deep work is too heavy for a small task
choosing between download methods for open-source distribution
entries.intent: Eliminate prerequisites from the onboarding critical path
entries.content: ZIP download as default onboarding path over git clone. Beginners don't have Git installed, creating a chicken-and-egg problem. The bootstrap script installs Git, so after setup they have it for future use. Git clone preserved as secondary path for experienced devs.

entries.reuse_signal: deciding default state for optional MCP servers
entries.intent: Prevent confusing error messages for new users while keeping the feature available
entries.content: kb-vectorstore disabled by default in template mcp.json. Setup.sh enables it after venv creation succeeds. Prevents error spam for users who skip vectorstore or haven't run setup yet. The system degrades gracefully to CSR-only without it.

entries.reuse_signal: benchmark failure that resolves naturally over time, expected behavior on fresh install
entries.intent: Preserve benchmark integrity while preventing false alarm fatigue
entries.content: When benchmark tests fail due to expected lifecycle behavior (e.g., seed data dominating fresh installs), add a knowledge entry explaining the expected behavior rather than modifying the benchmark to suppress the failure. The benchmark should reflect reality. Knowledge entries teach Nate why the failure is expected and when it resolves.

Chose uvx mcp-server-fetch over bash wrapper (run-fetch-server.sh) or direct python (secure-fetch.py) for the fetch MCP server. The bash wrapper broke in IDE due to path resolution. Direct python had cross-platform issues (python3 vs python). uvx is the standard pattern for all other awslabs servers and works identically in IDE and CLI.
entries.intent: Standardize MCP server invocation to prevent environment-specific failures
entries.reuse_signal: When choosing between wrapper scripts and direct uvx for MCP servers

entries.content: Production sessions and maintenance sessions are structurally different cognitive modes. Gaps form at the mode boundary: when building, you don't think about what needs updating elsewhere. The fix isn't 'remember to propagate' (behavioral, fails under load). It's a structural checkpoint after every production phase: 'what did I just create that other things reference?' Three sessions confirmed: spec produced without SDD propagation, SDD propagated without synergy check, benchmarks fixed without knowledge count updates.
entries.reuse_signal: completing a production artifact and switching to the next task without tracing downstream references
entries.intent: prevent maintenance debt from accumulating at cognitive mode boundaries

entries.content: Toolbelt uses three tiers: Tier 1 (daily drivers: fs_read, fs_write, execute_bash, grep, kb_search, get_current_time), Tier 2 (regular: fetch, use_subagent, code tool, kb_sync, email), Tier 3 (situational: AWS tools, SAM, pricing, delegate). The code tool was promoted from Tier 3 to Tier 2 because it has 7 AST operations we barely use — pattern_search and pattern_rewrite are more precise than grep for code. Thinking tool (chat.enableThinking) flagged for investigation: may conflict with kernel reasoning patterns (Route F, OBSERVE>QUESTION>DEDUCE).
entries.intent: Establish prescribed tool selection hierarchy and flag potential conflicts
entries.reuse_signal: When selecting tools for a task or evaluating new Kiro features for adoption

entries.content: Kernel Purity Check is a sub-check of the Destructive Action Gate, not a standalone gate. Rationale: it only fires when the target is Nathaniel.md (narrow scope), it's a classification step within an existing verification flow (not a new procedural interrupt), and adding standalone gates increases gate count which increases skip probability. The self-catch heuristic (proper nouns that aren't platform names or protocol filenames = probably knowledge) is lightweight enough to run inline.
entries.intent: Decide whether Kernel Purity is a new gate or a sub-check
entries.reuse_signal: When deciding whether a new check should be a standalone gate or embedded in an existing one

entries.content: GOLDEN specs that need updating (new KB entries, new patterns since spec date) should get direct edits, not full Deep Work re-runs. Deep Work is zero-to-GOLDEN. Re-running it on an existing GOLDEN spec wastes context on quality loops over unchanged sections, re-researches answered questions, and burns ceremony that doesn't add value. The right move: scan KB for entries after spec date, filter for relevant ones, update spec directly.
entries.intent: Determine whether to re-run Deep Work or directly update an existing GOLDEN spec
entries.reuse_signal: When a GOLDEN spec needs refreshing with new information

entries.content: Builder agents implement what the code spec says but don't cross-reference data file schemas with behavioral requirements. The section_aware flag existed in conflict_pairs.yaml and was specified in the SDD design doc, but the builder's PL101 implementation did global matching. The gap is at the boundary between 'code logic' and 'data-driven behavior'. Post-build review checklist should include: for every data file the code reads, verify each field in the schema is actually consumed by the code.
entries.intent: Improve builder agent output quality through targeted review
entries.reuse_signal: When reviewing builder output that reads config/data files, check if all data fields are consumed

entries.content: Always-on is the intended operating state with [local automation engine] installed. Nate shifts from session-based to persistent. Machine stays on, Kiro stays open. Shutdown is graceful degradation, not failure. Task queue (files) and job store (SQLite) are the persistence layer — both survive any crash. The system resumes, it doesn't restart from scratch. This enables the nightly cognition cycle, hypothesis testing, and the full Golden Ethos self-building loop.
entries.intent: Define the operating paradigm shift that Pulse enables for the Nate ecosystem
entries.reuse_signal: When planning features that depend on Nate being available, or designing crash recovery, or explaining the Pulse value proposition

entries.content: Template positioning should layer new value propositions on top of existing ones rather than replacing them. The 'what's more' pattern: keep the builder-audience pitch (framework for persistent AI personalities) as the lead, then add the market-gap insight (the structured knowledge extraction system every agent product is missing) as a second layer. This reaches two audiences without diluting either — builders see the framework, frustrated agent users see why this solves their 'now what?' problem. Replacing the positioning would risk losing the builder audience that already resonates with the technical pitch.
entries.intent: Determine how to incorporate market validation from podcast analysis into template positioning without losing existing audience
entries.reuse_signal: When adding a new value proposition to an existing product positioning — layer don't replace

entries.content: When a desired automation (file-event hooks) proves unreliable due to platform limitations, the better solution is often embedding the check into an existing gate or protocol rather than building a parallel system. The KB Coverage Check was added to the Destructive Action Gate (which already fires on every file op) rather than creating new hooks, scripts, queue files, or save steps. Result: zero new infrastructure, zero save overhead, catches agent-created files (which hooks never could), and works across all interfaces (CLI, IDE, terminal).
entries.reuse_signal: platform feature unreliable, need alternative automation approach, adding overhead vs embedding in existing flow
entries.intent: Choose the lightest integration point when adding new checks to the system
